citations.desc: Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC).
citations.narrative:
On 2/2/26, a lab member did not follow an approved protocol as written.
Records show a lab member administered Cerenia, an anti-nausea medication, to one ferret prior to undergoing a
surgical procedure. The approved protocol states “animals may be given one dose of Cerenia (1mg/kg, SQ) between 2
hours prior to the procedure and time of induction to reduce nausea”. Records show the medication was given IM not SQ
as intended, and that an incorrect dosage was calculated and given to the ferret. Once aware of the errors, the lab
member immediately contacted the Department of Comparative Medicine (DCM) veterinarians, and the ferret was
examined and showed no signs of distress. The ferret received additional health exams over the next two hours before
the lab was given clearance to proceed, and the ferret recovered from the surgery without any health concerns.
Failure to follow the approved protocol and making unapproved changes does not allow the IACUC to review these
changes as required and ensure the animals involved do not experience negative impacts to their health and well-being.
The IACUC must ensure that all significant changes regarding the care and use of animals in ongoing activities are
reviewed and approved prior to implementation.
Corrective action was implemented prior to this inspection.
To be corrected from this day forward, June 2, 2026

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care.
citations.narrative:
Lab personnel failed to inform the Division of Comparative Medicine (DCM) veterinarians of health concerns of one Naked
Mole Rat (NMR) chip id #074-887-097 in a timely manner. A lab member weighed the NMR on 3/5/26 and did not report
its significant weight loss, but, DCM veterinarians were contacted by lab personnel on 3/10/26 when it was noticed the
particular NMR was not doing well.
According to facility records, on 3/10/26 the DCM completed a veterinary check of the NMR colonies and NMR chip id
#074-887-097 was found “severely underweight, cold and unresponsive”. The veterinarian immediately provided
supportive care, but ultimately, the NMR was euthanized. Upon further review of the lab’s previous weights over a few
months, it was noted that this NMR had been losing weight and exceeded acceptable weight loss criteria based on
University Committee on Animal Resources (UCAR) and DCM humane end points. According to the protocol, once the
lab noticed the animal was losing greater than 10% of its body weight, the DCM veterinarians should have been notified
for consultation. By the time the DCM veterinarian had seen the NMR in March, it had lost more than 20% of its weight
from a December 2025 weighing.
Failure to inform the attending veterinarian of medical concerns in a timely manner may cause unrelieved pain, distress or
unnecessary discomfort to the animals.
Each research facility shall establish and maintain programs of adequate veterinary care that includes daily observation of
all animals to assess their health and well-being; Provided, however, That daily observation of animals may be
accomplished by someone other than the attending veterinarian; and Provided, further, That a mechanism of direct and
frequent communication is required so that timely and accurate information on problems of animal health, behavior, and
well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian.
To be corrected from this day forward June 2, 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representatives.
n

narrative:
2.31(c)(7) 
   
Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC). 
On 2/2/26, a lab member did not follow an approved protocol as written. 
Records show a lab member administered Cerenia, an anti-nausea medication, to one ferret prior to undergoing a 
surgical procedure. The approved protocol states “animals may be given one dose of Cerenia (1mg/kg, SQ) between 2

as intended, and that an incorrect dosage was calculated and given to the ferret. Once aware of the errors, the lab 
member immediately contacted the Department of Comparative Medicine (DCM) veterinarians, and the ferret was 
examined and showed no signs of distress. The ferret received additional health exams over the next two hours before 
the lab was given clearance to proceed, and the ferret recovered from the surgery without any health concerns.  
Failure to follow the approved protocol and making unapproved changes does not allow the IACUC to review these 
changes as required and ensure the animals involved do not experience negative impacts to their health and well-being. 
The IACUC must ensure that all significant changes regarding the care and use of animals in ongoing activities are 
reviewed and approved prior to implementation. 
Corrective action was implemented prior to this inspection.  
To be corrected from this day forward, June 2, 2026 
2.33(b)(3)  Critical
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care. 
Lab personnel failed to inform the Division of Comparative Medicine (DCM) veterinarians of health concerns of one Naked 
Mole Rat (NMR) chip id #074-887-097 in a timely manner. A lab member weighed the NMR on 3/5/26 and did not report 
its significant weight loss, but, DCM veterinarians were contacted by lab personnel on 3/10/26 when it was noticed the 
particular NMR was not doing well.  
According to facility records, on 3/10/26 the DCM completed a veterinary check of the NMR colonies and NMR chip id 
#074-887-097 was found “severely underweight, cold and unresponsive”. The veterinarian immediately provided 
supportive care, but ultimately, the NMR was euthanized. Upon further review of the lab’s previous weights over a few 
months, it was noted that this NMR had been losing weight and exceeded acceptable weight loss criteria based on 
University Committee on Animal Resources (UCAR) and DCM humane end points. According to the protocol, once the 
lab noticed the animal was losing greater than 10% of its body weight, the DCM veterinarians should have been notified 
for consultation. By the time the DCM veterinarian had seen the NMR in March, it had lost more than 20% of its weight

from a December 2025 weighing.  
Failure to inform the attending veterinarian of medical concerns in a timely manner may cause unrelieved pain, distress or 
unnecessary discomfort to the animals. 
Each research facility shall establish and maintain programs of adequate veterinary care that includes daily observation of 
all animals to assess their health and well-being; Provided, however, That daily observation of animals may be 
accomplished by someone other than the attending veterinarian; and Provided, further, That a mechanism of direct and 
frequent communication is required so that timely and accurate information on problems of animal health, behavior, and 
well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian. 
To be corrected from this day forward June 2, 2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representatives.
